title: Repainting a radiator
subtitle: Strip, prime, and recoat a steel panel radiator with a heat-resistant finish
excerpt: How to repaint a steel panel radiator without leaving brush marks or yellowed paint. Covers cold-down and isolating, surface prep, the choice between brushed and spray radiator paint, and the cure period before turning the heating back on.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A water- or solvent-based paint formulated to stay white under sustained heat. Standard wall and woodwork paints yellow at 60 to 80 celsius; radiator paint holds its colour up to around 95 celsius.
A resin-impregnated muslin cloth that lifts dust from a sanded surface before painting. The standard finishing step between sanding and painting; runs through one cloth per radiator.

content.text: A panel radiator repaints cleanly once or twice in its life, more if the original factory finish was poor. The job needs a cold radiator, fully isolated and drained at the surface, and a paint specifically formulated for heat: standard emulsion or eggshell yellows within a season.
Radiator paint
is the only sensible choice.
Step 1, turn the heating off and let the radiator cool

Turn the central heating off at the programmer. Wait until the radiator is fully cold to the hand: half an hour after a short run, two hours after a long winter session. Painting onto warm steel flashes the solvent and lifts the finish. There is no rush; the heating stays off through the whole job and the cure.
Step 2, clean the surface

Wash the radiator with sugar soap or warm water with a small amount of washing-up liquid. Get the back face as well as the front and the top edges. Dust collects on the top fins and inside the panel grilles; a soft brush or vacuum nozzle reaches them. Rinse with clean water and allow to dry fully.
Step 3, key the existing finish

Rub the whole surface with 240 grit sandpaper on the sanding block, then 320 grit. The goal is a matt finish, not bare metal. Any rust pits or paint chips get spot-treated with a rust-converter primer before the topcoat. Wipe the radiator down with a
tack rag
to lift the sanding dust.
Step 4, mask the wall and the valves

Run low-tack masking tape on the wall behind the radiator, around the brackets, and down both pipe runs. Mask the thermostatic valve head and the lockshield cap completely; paint on a TRV head clogs the spring. Lay a dust sheet under the radiator.
Step 5, brush the back and the top, then roll the front

Brush the back face first with the 50 mm brush, working through the gap between the radiator and the wall with a long-handled radiator brush if the gap is tight. Brush the top edge and the side ends next. Switch to the mini roller for the flat front panel. Roll in a single direction with even pressure; a back-and-forth stroke leaves laps. Two thin coats hold the colour better than one heavy coat.
Step 6, recoat after the first dries

Wait the recoat time on the paint tin, typically four to six hours for water-based, six to twelve for solvent-based. Light-sand with 320 grit between coats only if the first coat sits with visible texture. Apply the second coat in the same order as the first.
Step 7, cure before turning the heat back on

Leave the heating off for at least 24 hours after the second coat dries. A cure period under heat would soften the new paint and trap brush marks; a full cold cure sets the film. Pull the masking tape while the paint is still slightly soft, before it bonds to the dried film on the surface.
Variations

Aerosol radiator paint gives a finish closer to factory. Lift the radiator off the wall, or isolate and drain it. Set it on trestles in a well-aired space. Spray in light passes. The trade-off is fumes and overspray. A brushed finish on a panel radiator left on the wall is fine for most homes.
Care

A radiator repaint lasts five to ten years before any yellowing or chipping returns. Dust the top fins with the heating off once a season; trapped dust bakes onto the finish under heat and shortens the life.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The new paint yellowed within a season
attrs.cause: Standard emulsion or eggshell was used instead of dedicated radiator paint, or a solvent-based topcoat went over a water-based primer with no compatibility check.
attrs.fix: Strip the yellowed coat with a heat-stable stripper, key the surface, and recoat with a dedicated radiator paint. Match primer and topcoat (both water-based or both solvent-based).

attrs.symptom: Brush marks visible in the dried finish
attrs.cause: The paint was applied too thickly in one coat, or laid off in multiple directions before tacking.
attrs.fix: Sand back to flat with 240 then 320 grit, dust off, and apply two thin coats with a mini roller. Lay off in one direction only and stop reworking once the paint tacks.

attrs.symptom: The TRV head is stiff or stuck after the job
attrs.cause: Paint or solvent dripped onto the TRV spring through the mask.
attrs.fix: Remove the TRV head (single nut at the base), clean the spring and the brass body with a soft brush, wipe with a clean rag, refit. Replace the head if cleaning does not free it.
